officialSources.title: Files you can store in Google Drive
evidence: Google documents spreadsheet cell and column limits for Sheets-created, Sheets-converted, Excel-imported, and CSV-imported spreadsheets.
Google documents Connected Sheets row and cell limits separately from ordinary Sheets file limits.

officialSources.title: Use both Excel and Sheets: Best practices
evidence: Google documents Sheets as appropriate for collaboration when datasets fit within the documented Sheets cell limit.
Google documents continuing in Excel when the dataset is greater than the documented Sheets cell limit or needs Excel-only capabilities.

officialSources.title: Learn how to optimize your data ingestion
evidence: Google documents copy/paste for small ingestion and import functions for smaller dynamic datasets.
Google documents Connected Sheets and BigQuery as the path for very large CSV ingestion when direct Sheets import is too large.

officialSources.title: Learn how to improve Sheets performance
evidence: Google documents that edits can trigger background calculations and visible progress while users continue editing.
Google documents performance guidance around repeated expressions, volatile functions, and conditional formatting at scale.

officialSources.title: Optimize your data references to improve Sheets performance
evidence: Google documents using same-spreadsheet references and closed ranges to improve performance on large datasets.
Google documents that open ranges can force Sheets to inspect many rows even when the used subset is small.

officialSources.title: Excel specifications and limits
evidence: Microsoft documents Excel worksheet dimensions, workbook resource limits, calculation limits, and PivotTable limits.
Microsoft documents workbook and Data Model scale as memory and system-resource bounded in supported 64-bit environments.

officialSources.title: Tips for improving Excel's performance
evidence: Microsoft documents performance work across calculation, lookup, memory allocation, copy/paste, undo, scrolling, filtering, file open, and programmability.
Microsoft documents user-facing guidance for improving performance on heavy workbooks.

limitations: This artifact captures current official public documentation and published scale guidance, not a live timing run inside Google Sheets or Microsoft Excel.
The comparison proves direct external large-workbook scale evidence exists; it does not claim bilig is 10x faster than Google Sheets or Microsoft Excel.
bilig's checked large-workbook SLO currently covers 100k and 250k materialized-cell sessions, not every incumbent maximum-limit scenario.

dimensions.title: Published Grid And Workbook Limits
biligEvidence: The large-workbook SLO scorecard runs committed 100k and 250k materialized-cell load and warm-start contracts.
The dominance scorecard keeps large-workbook scale partial until max-limit and live incumbent timing artifacts exist.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google publishes hard Sheets cell and column limits for created, converted, Excel-imported, and CSV-imported spreadsheets.
Google's Excel-and-Sheets guidance treats datasets beyond the Sheets cell limit as a reason to remain in Excel.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft publishes Excel worksheet dimensions and memory/resource-bounded workbook limits.
Microsoft publishes calculation, PivotTable, selection, and Data Model limits relevant to large workbook behavior.
dimensions.comparisonResult: bilig now records official incumbent scale limits beside its executable 100k and 250k SLO evidence, while keeping the max-scale dominance claim open.
residualLimitations: bilig has not yet published a committed product maximum that exceeds the incumbent limits by 10x.
The published incumbent limits are not timing measurements.

dimensions.title: Large Data Ingestion And Load Guidance
biligEvidence: The SLO scorecard checks cold core snapshot load and browser worker warm start for the committed large-workbook corpuses.
The import/export fidelity scorecard separately covers supported CSV/XLSX semantics so file compatibility is not used as a proxy for large-workbook load speed.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google documents different ingestion paths for small copy/paste, import functions, and very large CSV ingestion through Connected Sheets and BigQuery.
Google publishes Sheets and Connected Sheets limits that constrain how much data can be loaded directly into Sheets experiences.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft documents workbook and Data Model scale limits that depend on available memory and system resources.
Microsoft documents performance improvements and guidance covering file open, filtering, scrolling, and workbook-heavy workflows.
dimensions.comparisonResult: bilig has executable local SLO load evidence and explicit separation from import/export fidelity, while Google and Microsoft publish mature large-data paths and resource boundaries.
residualLimitations: bilig still needs native Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel load/import timing artifacts for the same corpuses.
The external docs do not provide controlled p95 timings for comparable hardware or tenants.

dimensions.title: Large Workbook Performance Behavior
biligEvidence: The SLO scorecard enforces p95 budgets for 100k and 250k materialized-cell load, worker warm start, visible edit patching, and headed browser frame pacing.
The headed Playwright contracts verify smooth browse and bounded visible mutation behavior against the large workbook corpuses.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google documents background recalculation and performance risks from volatile functions, repeated expressions, conditional formats, import functions, and open ranges.
Google documents practical modeling guidance to reduce CPU use, improve stability, and speed up large-dataset work.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft documents performance work and tips across calculation, lookup, memory, copy/paste, undo, scrolling, filtering, file open, and programmability.
Microsoft documents many calculation and workbook limits as bounded by memory or worksheet dimensions.
dimensions.comparisonResult: bilig has machine-checked local p95 budgets for its current large-workbook corpuses; incumbent docs show scale and tuning behavior but do not substitute for direct timing evidence.
residualLimitations: The comparison remains documentation-backed rather than a live browser/desktop p95 benchmark against Sheets and Excel.
bilig needs larger corpus tiers and hosted collaboration scale so the SLO evidence spans more of the objective.

dimensions.title: Live Incumbent Timing Gap
biligEvidence: The dominance scorecard still blocks a blanket 10x claim unless direct Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel evidence exists for every objective category.
The large-workbook SLO scorecard records official-docs comparison evidence but keeps direct incumbent live timing as an explicit remaining gap.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google's official docs provide product limits and performance guidance but not a controlled benchmark trace for bilig's checked corpuses.
Google Sheets performance guidance is workload-modeling advice rather than a downloadable timing baseline.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft's official docs provide product limits and performance guidance but not a controlled benchmark trace for bilig's checked corpuses.
Excel performance guidance describes optimized areas and user practices rather than a comparable p95 benchmark artifact.
dimensions.comparisonResult: The external large-workbook evidence gap is narrowed from missing docs to missing live incumbent timing, which keeps the category honest and actionable.
residualLimitations: A future live timing artifact must run the same corpus families in Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel under documented hardware, browser, tenant, and workbook settings.
Until then, this category remains partial evidence rather than a 10x product win.
